segments.text: And Michael Kilkelly joining us.
Get set.
Karen,
we have 10 minutes with you this morning. That includes time for questions.
Pass it over to you now.
Thank you. Mauri ora koutou.
We're both members of the National Employment Law Committee of the New Zealand Law
Society, Te Kāhui Ture o Aotearoa.
I'm a barrister in Wellington. I've got over 30 years experience in employment
law, which covers two Holidays Acts and two decades in large
law firms. And Michael is an associate with Meredith Connell in Auckland, and
prior to that he was a judge's clerk in the Employment Court in Auckland.
The approach taken by the New Zealand Law Society when commenting on proposed
legislation is a neutral one in terms of policy.
So we're seeking to provide you with feedback on the workability
of the legislation, identifying potential issues in drafting.
And as lawyers, our natural
review mechanism is to think about how we might explain it to our clients.
Mm-hmm.
Our written submission makes a number of comments that are directed to the
predictability and certainty of the proposed legislation, but we'll focus
only on three of those points today, and then leave a few minutes
for questions from you.
So I'll start with the standard hours point that we make in our submission.
The bill is premised on standard hours being specified in
an employment agreement roster or notional roster, and that's the
baseline or foundation for annual leave, public holidays, bereavement
leave, sick leave, domestic violence leave, et cetera.
So it's really important as a concept in this draft
legislation.
So just to start with a practical issue.
At the moment, standard hours isn't required in
employment agreements. The Employment Relations Act brings in more
flexibility than that, and it refers to a requirement to
include an indication of the arrangements relating to the times the employee is
to work. So you see some quite flexible and fluid clauses at the
moment in employment agreements.
So there's going to be some transition needed if we go with the standard hours
notion from what currently is quite flexible and where
many employment agreements won't have standard or set hours
specified, and it's not quite clear how that transition's going to be
addressed.
This leads to the second issue about standard hours, which is the bill requires
that, but doesn't provide a fallback
mechanism if the hours aren't specified in any of those
ways. So where the employer and employee simply don't agree and they stick with
their current employment agreement, what happens then?
The third issue is whether the standard hours truly reflect what the employee
works. And I listened earlier this morning to some of the industry submissions,
which have explained the implication of that for how
the time off part of leave is calculated.
For the Law Society, our concern is twofold.
First is that the standard hours might not reflect the current reality, but
second, the, shall we say, less honorable employers might deliberately
set low standard hours knowing that the employees will
happily voluntarily work additional hours-
Mm-hmm
... in order to boost their pay to that point.
But that means they don't have annual leave and the other actual time off
entitlements that accrue at the rate that they're actually working.
The second point I'm going to touch on is what we colloquially know as
time and a half, for payment on public holidays.
If I can be frank, the clause relating to that,
124, it's quite long. It has cross-references
in itself,
and references between some clauses that are quite difficult to follow.
It's one of those
clauses that you read and you wonder how you might actually explain that to anybody
in terms of how it's meant to work. So it is complicated.
The second point about it is that there are two
mechanisms for the time and a half.
So there's one for time,
and that's what the employer's required to pay the employee for each hour
worked. And then the half has a different calculation,
and that's greater of 50% of the employee's ordinary hourly
rate and any identifiable amount.
So you're doing one calculation for the time and then another one for the half.
And I'm by no means a payroll expert, but I think that's going to
complicate things as people try to put this in
practice.So that,
yeah, those calculation parts might not be the same, which risks the
calculations being very complex to do.
So I'll hand over to Michael now to talk about our final point,
which is the remediation issue.
Thank you, Karen, and good morning, everyone.
So I'm just going to speak to you briefly about the proposed
remediation process for outstanding liabilities under the Holidays Act,
and those provisions are at Clause 156 in Schedule 3 of the bill.
It's proposed that that process will have retrospective effect and could be used to
remediate underpayments and payment failures, which, for whatever
reason, have occurred up to four years prior to the bill receiving the Royal
Assent.
We're concerned that the material relating to the bill does not discuss whether the
retrospective provisions are appropriate.
There does not seem to be any consideration of whether
retrospectivity can adversely impact either employers or
employees. For example, by reducing or extinguishing some
employees' statutory entitlements.
Second, whether the extent of the retrospectivity is appropriate or if it
requires adjustment,
and third, whether this process could upset
or lead to relitigation of settled matters.
We also have concerns as to whether it is appropriate to prescribe
the substantive content
of those retrospective provisions through regulations
rather than through primary legislation, because regulations would not be
subject to the same level of public and parliamentary
scrutiny.
Those concerns are heightened as the bill does not require the minister to
undertake public consultation on the draft
regulations in circumstances where statutory entitlements are
impacted.
Finally, we have concerns about regulations that would specify a
minimum amount payable, a term that is
in that undefined category that I just spoke about, will be defined through the
regulations, because the regulations would not be subject to the same
level of parliamentary and public scrutiny.
This would mean an employer would not be required to make a remediation payment to
an employee whose entitlement is below this threshold.
It raises fairness concerns as the threshold could bar an
employee from receiving a payment they are otherwise entitled to receive,
no matter how small the amount. Those concerns are heightened
because we do not know at what level that threshold will be set or how it will be
calculated.
And a similar concern arises around the concept of reasonable compensation,
given that also appears to contemplate something less than their existing
statutory entitlements.
We advise you to
seek advice on those matters and to consider whether amendments are
necessary to address those concerns.
I'll hand over for questions if there's any time.
Thank you very much. We do have a short amount of time,
but we do have one question. I was going to say, it doesn't appear we have any.
We have a question from Jan Tinetti.
Thank you. Thank you, Madam Chair, and thank you very much for your
submission.
It kind of backs up, in many respects, what some of the
industry bodies have been saying, as you said.
Just one thing I want to get there, you talked about if there
is not so savory employers as such and what
they might do in this situation. To put it in simple terms, would
that be... And I'm trying to get my head around this too.
Does that mean that they could be incentivizing
part-time and casualization workers in this particular case
because it could mean that they're paying out less in the annual leave?
To answer that vaguely, yes, possibly.
But also you could have full-time employees
who
are put on, say, 35 or 30 hours a week.
It may be that you're thinking of that as part-time, which is probably right, but
work greater hours than that. So they might, say,
have the standard hours of 30 or 35 in their agreement, but
work much more than that and on a regular basis.
And
I think there is a possibility of
some, and I'm hesitating to use this word, but some abuse of the
system,
particularly by unscrupulous employers who are trying to limit how
much time off people actually have, particularly if they have to bring in
replacement employees at a higher cost.
Great. Thank you.
Thank you for that. And a question from Lawrence Yunane.
Thank you, Madam Chair. Just a very quick question regarding the
standard hours as well. Do you have any data or
what is your understanding of the current average
hours that employees actually work a week?
And potentially, is it greater than the standard hours that
we're seeing now? The reason I ask this question is, we
hear anecdotally, but I want to check if there's any data to say that people are
working longer and longer hours that are
unseen or unaccounted for in their contract.
That's not data that the Law Society would hold.
So the answer to your question is no, we don't have that data.
It might be held by the
Victoria University Employment Relations Centre.
They do an annual analysis of collective agreements and what provisions are in
those. So the data may be somewhere, but it's not held by us, I'm afraid.
Thank you. We might look into that. Thank you so much.
All right. Thank you very much. We appreciate your participation in the
process and your very constructive and helpful submission.
So thank you again.
Thank you.
We'll move on now to of a similar theme, Simpson Grierson.
We have Rachel Judge and Mike Mercer joining us.
Rachel and Mike, we have 10 minutes with you this morning, and that
includes time for questions.
Thank you. Tena koutou katoa. I am Rachel Judge, a
partner at Simpson Grierson, and I'm presenting with my colleague,
Mike Mercer, Senior Associate. We have both been
heavily involved in advising on the current Holidays Act and
assisting clients through their remediation processes.
And we thank you for the opportunity to present our oral submission today on
behalf of Simpson Grierson. This submission has been
informed by feedback from a number of our clients and
also, in line with what Karen's just spoken to, our own
observations as lawyers as to where complexities may
arise based on the wording of the current bill.
At the outset, I do want to note that Simpson Grierson
supports the core objectives of the bill.
We have been actively advocating for many of these
changes for a number of years. In particular, the move to an
hours-based accrual model and the simplification of
leave payment calculations. That said, there are
some aspects of the bill that are likely to pose some compliance
issues for employers, and particularly those with
complex workforces. So we do consider that there
is room for the Bill to potentially include some options to
address some of those issues. And we wish to comment on three
key issues from our written submissions where we have made
recommendations. Those are the notional roster,
the mandatory standard hours versus additional
hours leave compensation payment approach, which we'll refer to
as LCP, and the issue of strike action during the
transitional period.
So just turning to the notional roster framework to
start with. This applies to determine leave
entitlements where an employee's standard hours or days
of work cannot be readily identified.
And while the policy intent is understood, we submit that
the framework as drafted may not be workable in
practice for many employers. Clause nine, for
example, requires that a notional roster is agreed
in writing and kept up to date. In workplaces where
hours are variable or change frequently, that will be
difficult, if not impossible, to apply.
The combination of a requirement for agreement and then
continual updating risks continuous negotiation,
creating significant administrative burden and also
employee relations issues flowing from all of that.
For large employers, and particularly those with unionized workforces,
this is unlikely to be practicable.
There is also a level of uncertainty in the role a notional
roster is intended to play. Although it's described as
notional, the provisions do require it to
reflect actual working patterns to a certain extent and to be
revised as those patterns change.
So this risks blurring the distinction between a genuine
notional construct and an operational roster,
and in our view, risks undermining the purpose of introducing
a simplified mechanism in the first place.
We recognize that there is provision at clauses 10 and 11
for a labor inspector to determine a notional roster where
agreement cannot be reached, but we consider that
reliance on labor inspector determinations as the sole fallback
is likely to result in increased disputes,
delay, and litigation. So on this point, we
submit that the framework would be materially improved by
introducing an express alternative mechanism
where clause nine could be amended to provide that where it is
not reasonably practicable for an employer and employee to
agree a notional roster. That agreement is not
required as a precondition to determining those standard hours for
leave purposes. And in line with that, that clause
10 is amended to allow an employer, without labor
inspector intervention, to determine a notional
roster by applying some form of agreed averaging
method. And we're hearing from our clients that if some form of
rolling average were applied, such as the 13-week average,
that that's something that could be workable for them going forward.
Mm-hmm.
I'll now hand over to Mike Mercer to discuss the
further two points we wanted to raise.
Thank you, Rachel, and kia ora koutou katoa.
Further, the two points I want to speak to, the first relates to the leave
compensation approach, and like our learned friends from the New Zealand Law
Society, we address the mechanism for additional hours being
paid with via the leave compensation payment.
While we understand this is intended to provide certainty and to avoid the
accumulation of large leave liabilities, there are circumstances
where hours of work will fluctuate and it could create
some outcomes for an employer that are not consistent with the Bill and operational
difficulties. In particular, the structure of the leave
compensation payment will create incentive for employees to
favor being paid in a cash loading rather than accruing leave
and resist agreement to standard hours.
What we're hearing from clients who are really concerned about their ability to
agree with standard hours with their employees is that given the
attractiveness of leave compensation payment, you could end up
in a situation that is different to what was being discussed
previously, with actually employees, all time including, who
would prefer to end up on low standard hours with large additional
hours that would provide the disincentive to them taking sick
leave when needed and significantly receive their reduced pay when they do
so.The bill doesn't currently allow for alternative approaches
that may produce the same or better outcome for employees.
In practice, we are hearing from clients who have a strong preference to accrue
leave on all hours worked, including additional hours,
while others may prefer the pay-as-you-go model of the
compensation payment.
Either approach would be broadly neutral in effect for employees.
They either get leave paid at the time or the ability to take it
later. In our view, there is scope to deliver on the intent of the
bill while allowing greater flexibility in how leave entitlements are
delivered without jeopardizing employee protections or entitlements.
We therefore recommend that clauses 129 and
132 of the bill be amended to allow employers, as
an alternative to paying leave compensation on additional hours,
to accrue leave on all hours worked in addition,
including those additional hours at their election.
The second point we wish to raise relates to strike action during the
transitional period. It is inevitable that there will be areas of
disagreement during the transition process, given the new leave framework
will require significant changes to existing employment agreements,
payroll systems, and operational practices.
We are concerned that without clear limits, disputes arising solely from
the application of the transitional provisions may become the subject of
strike action or lockouts.
There is precedent that unions and employees cannot participate in
unlawful industrial action, even when it otherwise relates to
bargaining, if the issue in dispute arises from a legislatively
defined process.
By way of example,
the Fair Pay Agreements Act in 2022, which has
since been repealed, included express restrictions on industrial
action in relation to fair pay bargaining.
We therefore recommend that the bill be amended to provide that disputes arising
solely from the application or operation of the transitional provisions cannot
be subject to industrial action during the transitional
period. This could be achieved by either adopting a similar
approach to that in the former Fair Pay Agreement Act, and in
this context, disagreements about the conversion, reconciliation, or technical
application of the transition rules should be treated as procedural
matters and resolved through interpretation, guidance, or dispute resolution
mechanisms by the employment institutions rather than
industrial action. Thank you, Madam Chair and members of the Select Committee.
We are prepared to take any questions at this time.
We have one minute, and it doesn't appear that we do.
And can I just say, it is not a reflection of the lack of
interest. I have to make that very clear.
Very good.
You always put forward an incredibly-
Very good
... comprehensive and helpful submission.
Yeah.
And I thank you for that, and I know that our officials would-
Excellent. Thank you
... thank you for that, too.
Thank you.
Very good. Thank you.
Thank you.
So we'll move on now to our next submitter.
Thank you.
Thank you. We have Madfly Limited Trading.
We have Sheryn Waltu.
Hopefully, I'm pronouncing your surname correctly.
That's all right.
Enterprise and Staffing Innovations New Zealand, if that makes more sense
to you.
Apologies. Having a few technical issues there. I hope you can hear me.
That's okay. Easy. You got-
Right. So apologies.
EasyNZ, sorry. Let's just get that right. I happen to see in your logo.
Sorry. It is actually, it's a trading name of Madfly Limited.
So, thank you, Madam Chairman and members of the committee.
Thank you for the opportunity to appear here today and to speak on, uh, to EasyNZ's
written submission on the Employment Leave Bill.
My name is Sheryn Waltu, and I'm the managing director appearing on behalf of
Enterprise and Staffing Innovations NZ, otherwise known as EasyNZ.
By way of background, EasyNZ provides practical employment relations and
HR support to businesses across New Zealand.
A big part of our work is helping clients with the operational employment issues
that come up every day in real workplaces.
Things like leave, payroll, payroll interpretation, rostering process, and
documentation. These are matters that can look quite straightforward on paper, but
if they're handled incorrectly, they can very quickly turn into disputes.
A large proportion of our clients are small to medium-sized businesses.
For those businesses, getting employment processes wrong is not just frustrating,
it's expensive, distracting, and disruptive.
Most do not have in-house legal teams or large HR functions,
if at all. They're trying to do the right thing, but they need the law to be clear,
and they need it to be workable. When disputes do arise,
even a claim with little or no merit can still cost up
to $10,000 to defend in cost alone before you
factor in management time, stress, and disruption to the business.
So from our perspective, clarity in employment legislation really matters.
If the law is too technical, too uncertain, or too hard to apply in day-to-day
operations, the consequences are very real.
With that in mind, our overall position on this is that we support
the direction of reform, but we say the bill still needs refinement.
We support the objective behind the bill.
The current leave framework is too complicated.
It's been difficult for employers to understand and difficult for payroll systems
to properly apply, and it's led to too many errors and too much
uncertainty, so reform is needed.
We support the intention of creating a simpler and clearer leave system, but
our key message today is that simplification should not come at the cost of
fairness, operational workability, or new compliance risks.
In our view, the bill makes some very positive changes, but there are still several
areas where it needs tightening so it does not simply replace one set of problems
with another.
So there are parts of the bill that we clearly support.
We support pro-rated sick leave accruing from the first day of employment.
That's easier to understand, and it better reflects service over time.
We support annual leave accruing from day one.
That's much easier to explain than the current distinction between accrued leave
and annual leave becoming available only after 12 months.
And we support clearer rules around public holidays and otherwise working days.
Greater clarity in that area should reduce uncertainty and reduce the risk of
underpayment issues.So this is not a submission opposing
reform, quite the opposite. We support reform.
We just want to make sure the reform is genuinely workable in practice.
Our biggest concern is the 12.5% leave compensation payment for
hours above minimum contracted hours.
Mm.
This is probably our most significant concern.
We're concerned about the proposal to require a leave compensation payment of at
least 12.5% for additional hours and casual hours.
That may appear simple at a high level, but in practice, it creates real problems
for businesses where workers have genuine ongoing employment and regular
patterns of work, but may work more than their guaranteed minimum hours.
This is a very common arrangement, especially in small to medium businesses.
In many cases, businesses will guarantee, for example, 30 hours a week,
but regularly offer more. The reason for that is practical.
In sectors such as agriculture and construction, the amount of work available
is often heavily dependent on weather conditions and changing operational
demands. Businesses are trying to provide employees with a reliable
minimum, while still retaining the flexibility to offer more hours when
conditions allow. So a person may be contracted for 30 hours, but
often work 40 when the weather is favorable and work can proceed, and in things
like construction, that could actually be even more, so you might work more hours
in the summer than you do in the winter.
That
does not make them casual in the ordinary sense.
Our concern is that if every hour above guaranteed minimum automatically attracts a
12.5% leave payment, that creates a number of problems.
So it creates more payroll complexity, not less.
It increases labor cost immediately.
It may reduce the employee's ability to build up meaningful paid leave over time,
and it may encourage a system where leave is effectively cashed out as work
happens, instead of being preserved as time away from work.
From our perspective, that's not just a payroll issue, it's also a wellbeing
issue.
Mm.
So our recommendation is that the bill should draw a clearer distinction between
genuine casual work and ordinary additional hours worked in
ongoing employment.
Where there is a real ongoing employment, there should be a pathway for leave to
accrue rather than requiring automatic pay-as-you-go treatment.
So the bill also needs to be clearer and more practical, there needs to be more
practical treatment of casual employment.
Closely linked to the 12.5% leave compensation payment is the question of
casual work.
One of the real issues for employers is knowing when a worker who starts out as a
casual has in reality become regular.
Mm.
That matters because it affects payroll treatment, leave treatment,
legal risks, and fairness. If the bill is going to rely on a separate model
for casual employees, then it needs to be much clearer about when
someone is genuinely casual and when they're no longer casual.
Mm.
Otherwise, we're just creating a new uncertainty in place of an old one.
So our recommendation is that the bill include either a clearer statutory test
or a review point, so that where work becomes regular and predictable, the employee
moves into the ordinary accrual work frame or framework,
sorry.
The bill should also avoid creating unfairness between casual and permanent
workers. We're also concerned about parity on this one.
If a casual worker receives 12.5% upfront, but a permanent worker
accrues leave that only becomes valuable when it's actually taken, then there's a
real possibility of inequitable outcomes between different categories
of workers.
We're not suggesting casual workers should be disadvantaged. That's not our point.
Our point is that simplification should still produce fair outcomes
across the workforce. The committee should test carefully whether the
structure of the bill creates unintended imbalance between workers who may be
doing similar work, but under different employment arrangements.
So next we have KiwiSaver and commission need to be expressly clarified.
We also think the bill should be clearer on some points around the remuneration
issues. The first is KiwiSaver. The treatment of KiwiSaver in relation to
leave compensation payments should be set out clearly in the legislation.
Mm.
If it's not, employers and payroll providers will take different approaches, and
that inconsistency will create exactly the kind of confusion this reform is trying
to fix.
The second is commission and non-discretionary incentive payments.
A simpler model, sorry, simpler leave model should not result in regular commission
income being left out of leave calculations where that income is a normal
part of the employee's earnings. If that happens,
employees in commission-based or incentive-based roles may end up worse off
when taking leave, and that creates a real disincentive to taking leave at all.
That would be an unfair outcome and would undermine the broader purpose of leave
entitlements. So we say the bill should expressly preserve the inclusion of
regular non-discretionary payment and similar payments, or provide a
mechanism that properly captures them.
The variable hour work is still not fully resolved, especially around
day-based leave and notional rosters.
Our final point is that the bill does not yet fully resolve the practical issues
that arise for workers with variable hours and variable days.
Mm.
We remain concerned about day-based leave, particularly bereavement leave
and family violence leave.
Mm.
Where people do not work the same pattern each week, there can be uncertainty about
what counts as a day, how part days should be treated, and how leave should apply
across changing rosters.
We're also concerned about notional
rosters. At the moment, the bill does not seem to give enough practical certainty
about when a notional roster is required, what information is needed to create
one, and when it should be reviewed.
Without that clarity, there's a real risk of more inconsistency,
more confusion, and ultimately more disputes.
At the very least, we think that the legislation or supporting regulations
should include clearer guidance and practical worked examples
before the bill comes into force.
So in closing, our message is a straightforward one.
We support the goal of this bill. We support day one accrual for
sick leave and annual leave, and we support clearer public holiday rules.
But we ask the committee to refine the bill so it does not simplify shift
complexity from one part of the system to another.
Sorry, does not simply shift complexity. Sorry.
In particular, we ask the committee to look carefully at the 12.5% leave
compensation rule, the treatment of casual work,
fairness between casual and permanent workers, KiwiSaver treatment,
commissioner and incentive payments, and the practical operation of day-based
leave and notional rosters. If these areas are improved, we think the bill
will be stronger, fairer, and much more workable in the real world.
Thank you for your time, and I'm happy to answer questions if you've got time.
Thank you very much, Sherylyn. We do believe-- I think
we've run out of time with you, we have, unfortunately.
But thank you so much for being part of this process, and I've got to say, a really
good submission, really helpful.
And I assume-
Thank you
... and assure you, sorry, that the
officials will be pleased too.
Oh, thank you very much.
Move on now to Essential HR. Mike Johnson,
who I believe is online.
Mike, there we are. Wonderful. Oh.
I came, and I went.
Yes.
That was a quick submission. There we go, Mike.
Ten minutes for you, includes time for questions, and we'll hand it over to you
now.
Tena koutou. Good morning. I'm Mike Johnson from Essential
HR,
and I want to thank you for giving me an opportunity to speak to you all.
Madam Chairman, and members of the committee, I'd like to start by saying how
impressed I am with the draft legislation.
It had a horrible starting place, being the Holidays Act, and so
many things to address, and it does,
from my point of view, I really sense some positive development moving ahead
from the Holidays Act,
and a very clear attempt to address a wide range of issues, things like casual
staff and the pro-rata-ing of sick leave from day one, dealing
with on-call and stuff like that. It's, yeah, raise my hat
to you all, and especially as you now try and process
everything that's coming at you in this consultation.
Now, I speak on behalf of SMEs.
I have some four to five hundred whom I provide HR advice to,
and I've been pleased that I can already go back to them and
confirm for many of them that their concerns will be addressed in the new
legislation, and there'll always be some, of course, that are not.
There is one, though, in particular, and it's the first and significant issue
that I referred to in my written submission,
that I want to address today, and I strongly suggest
you consider the particular recommendation that I'm going to make.
And the reality is that this is all about
enabling small businesses to comply with the legislation.
So I want you to start by picturing an SME.
It may be a small retail outlet, a cafe, a restaurant, roofing
contractor. There are any number of them whose workload
is dependent on the weather or on a daily varying
level of customer demand. We all know them, and these
businesses can thrive, but that's especially where staff are
working as a team. They dig deep sometimes.
They
leave early where that benefits the business.
They'll work a Saturday to help make up time.
And for these and many others, compliance with the Holidays Act was just a
nightmare.
There was no way a business owner could understand what was needed to comply,
so they relied on their payroll provider to do things
right. But we all know that at some stage, all payroll
systems were non-compliant with the Holidays Act.
And yet it was the SME who
bore the brunt and the cost of a labor inspector's
audit and its resulting penalties,
and it just didn't seem right.
For example, how many payroll systems that you know, do you think would cope
easily with a roster that has somebody working,
Wednesday, seven hours, Thursday, 10, Friday, 10,
Saturday, 14 and a half, Sunday, two, with
sleepovers in between each of them, then three days rest, and then going through
the whole thing again. It was a roster that effectively
is an eight-day revolving roster, but payroll systems just
couldn't cope with it.
It was almost not possible to comply
with the legislation.
We need something similar, something
simple rather, not similar, something simple to manage
what have become some of the most complex of rosters.
And while this bill has gone so far, it can easily go a step
further in removing complexity.
It's a real challenge when it comes to drafting
legislation, I know.
And I've just pulled out Section 123 of the bill, Section
2, Subsection 2, where "The employee's leave payment must not be
not less than the rate of leave payment for each relevant hour,
that the employee takes as a period of leave, or that he does not work on a
public holiday that would otherwise be a working day," et cetera.
Yet for most business owners, if you present them
with that, it's not that
it's wrong. It's not. It's correct.
But it's just that it leaves them saying, "Eh?"
The business owners are never going to get their heads around it.
For the SMEs that I had you picturing before, there's no
simple standard hours,
and the many additional hours may or may not be linked to an
availability clause,
because for many of them, it's actually just part of their commitment to their
jobs.
So we need a simpler way to collate dollars for leave payments
broadly,
and for ease of understanding and to avoid having to
rely on the payroll provider for compliance.
So please, can we have just a few very simple
rules built into this legislation?
The first one, for every dollar that you earn-You put
aside, or that somebody earns, you put aside another 12 and a half
cents for leave, eight and a half probably for annual leave, four for
sick leave.
For every hour worked, you accrue the 0.0769
hours plus 0.0385 hours.

supported by solidarity members here at YWCA, and a few of those are employers.
A couple of them have reached out to me because they've said, "Oh, we've
heard about how this is going to impact young workers, and we want to give our
workers, our employees more than the minimum.
Can we just give them extra five, 10 days," yada, yada, yada,
"or maintain the 10 days?" AndI was like,
"Yeah, except you're still going to have to do that conversion." And that was
really unclear to them,
and these employers, as well as these young workers.
There was a lot of
confusion, harder to find a pathway forward. It's less clear, in short.
And my follow-up question is the comment she made around
how
people are out there, they're told their casual workers are actually their
part-time workers. I wonder if you could give some concrete, specific
examples that would make it clearer for the committee.
Kia ora. Yeah, for sure.
So
we had an ice cream worker in one of our workshops a
couple of weeks ago, and this young worker had been working at this
ice cream shop for, I think it was nearly two and a half
years. They worked consistently every Saturday,
and
those hours would increase during the summer times, as you can imagine.
But they'd been working regularly, at least every Saturday, for over two
years. They were on a casual agreement.
When we described the working patterns, in our workshops, we go through and we talk
about employment types, and when we explain what a
part-time permanent employee looks like in comparison to a casual worker, this
young worker had this penny drop moment of, "Oh,
shoot, I'm supposed to be a part-time worker."
They were really scared when they learnt that, actually, the reality
of that means that their boss can just stop offering them shifts, and that
stability that they've gained over the past two years, that reliance on this income
would just instantly drop.
Sorry-
When-
Matariki, I must stop you there because we've got to move on to our next

to the process, and you making yourself available for us today, so
thank you.
Kia ora.
Thanks. Kia ora rā.
We move on now to Business South, who we have online.
We have Shona Brown, Katerina Slade, and Wilfred
Prell.
Good afternoon, members of the committee and Chair.
I'm Shona Brown, advocacy advisor at Business South,
and we are part of the Otago Chamber of Commerce
and
the shareholder of Business New Zealand.
We're presenting around 1,400 businesses across Otago and
Southland.
The Holidays Act has been a genuine pain point for our members for years,
with
examples like payroll errors, costly remediation,
and real uncertainty about whether they're getting it right.
So we would like this reform to succeed and support
the direction of the bill.
I'm sitting here not as a technical expert.
Our role is to bring the lived experience of businesses on the
ground, and our submission is built directly from member
consultation.
We were fortunate to have Minister Van Velden join a members-only
webinar only recently to walk through the bill and take
questions, which has directly shaped what we've raised in our
submission. So rather than speak to the content in our submission,
I've brought along a member. I've got Katerina Slade
and Wilfred Prell from PurePak.
They're based in Cromwell in Central Otago.
They're exactly the kind of business this bill needs to work for.
They have a seasonal workforce with variable hours and
weather-dependent operations.
They can speak to both what's working in the bill and where the practical problems
sit, and possible solutions. I'd encourage the committee to
direct any detailed questions to both of them, as they're
far better placed than I am to bring those issues to life.
So thank you to the committee for your time, and I'll hand over to Katerina.
Thank you, Shona.
I'd like to introduce ourselves a little bit further.
We're a cherry-growing and pack house business in Cromwell.
Our harvest season runs specifically from around...
Well, not quite specifically, from the middle of December, and that
can shift around within a two-week period due to the high uncertainty of it.
The harvest period runs for about six weeks, sometimes seven.
We, at peak, employ 250 to
300 people to work both on the picking and harvest side, and in our
packing operation.
Many of our employees are backpackers on working holiday
visas. We've also got a reasonably good cohort of
returning semi-retired employees and a
few local school kids.
We support the bill's direction
overall, and there are a lot of great components to it that we
definitely agree with.
Our verbal submission will pull out the parts that are
concerning to us or that we think could be tweaked to work more
appropriately for seasonal employment.
The
first
kind of area of concern, I guess, is around the LCP and sick leave.
Obviously, if you reverse engineer it, it's based on a 10-day entitlement or the
equivalent thereof.
Firstly, that increases the cost to employers to the full 10-day
entitlement, which is more than what most permanent
employees would
cost, because many of them don't actually take their full entitlement.
It's there if they need it, and so it should be,
but it makes it
slightly weighted towards a casual.Secondly, and
probably most importantly, as a food handling organization where we are
processing cherries and there are 70 people kind of
coming into contact with the food.
Granted, there is a lot of PPE involved.
We strongly prefer that sick leave is
actually used for genuine illness to prevent people coming to
work when they're sick and coming into contact with cherries that are then going
out into the world for consumption.
So
paying out in cash that sick leave removes the incentive for an
employee to call in sick on the day when they're genuinely sick.
They may feel able to do work, but we still don't want them contaminating the
product.
So that's why we're against cashing out any kind of sick leave and would prefer
it's used.
Bereavement leave is another one that we're a little bit
worried about the potential for misuse. So...
Oh, actually, sorry, I've skipped ahead.
Yes. We'll talk about the uncertainty factor in the LCP.
So there's a bit of rounding or uncertainty factor woven into that, which
we'd just like to
raise that as an employer, we equally experience that uncertainty
factor. We can offer someone, when they're on a truly casual
contract, we offer them a shift, and they don't have to accept it.
So that uncertainty is experienced both by the employee because they don't know if
they're going to get offered a shift, and by the employer who doesn't know if the
employee is going to accept the shift, and we have to build that variability into
our business as well. So we don't support there being extra compensation
for that.
Bereavement leave,
particularly in a short employment period, there
is not that built-up trust as there is with a longer-term employment arrangement,
and it is open to misuse if
an employee, two days into their contract, there just needs to be some
kind of
ability to make a reasonable request to check the evidence so that the integrity of
the leave's purpose is protected.
The,
I guess, largest concern for us is the
assessment of an otherwise working day, and we really appreciate
the attempt to increase the
clarity around that. That is definitely needed.
The current
legislation and interpretation is a real significant source of
friction in our
workplace and I think in a lot of seasonal businesses.
So definitely can acknowledge and agree that clarity is needed.
Where the concern comes from is when we employ someone
in the middle of December, right when our harvest period starts for a six-week,
seven-week contract.
In that period, there's a disproportionate number of public
holidays
that fall.
And under the proposed assessment of looking at up to 13
weeks history,
but less if less exists,
you could end up in a situation where someone has only worked two days
in that period, and they decline every other shift that we offer them,
and they're entitled to then four days of
paid public holiday leave. So
yes, it would be very clear on what the entitlement is.
But in practicality, it's weighted too far.
Equally, on the other foot, the opposite could happen.
Someone could work every other day except that Friday and Saturday
beforehand and not be entitled to a single public holiday.
But they've actually worked more
than someone who might be. So
it's tricky. I can see it's really tricky.
A
potential suggestion which we've come up on how to
tackle this issue is in the situation where a worker has
a really short work history or less than 13
weeks,
is to assume that over a 12-month period, if they're doing casual work,
that they perhaps qualified for half of all the public holidays and
include that in the LCP and have no other
holiday entitlements.
That way, it's
clear what the entitlements are. You don't get it, you get an extra piece of
payment for the fact that
to compensate you for not being able to take the public holidays.
And it weights
it across the workforce at every time of the year.
It's not disproportionately hitting businesses that, like ours, which are
forced to harvest our crop during that summer period.
I believe that's...
Wilfred, did I miss anything?
Yeah, we just want the burden of cost to be fair,
more equally spread,
and to remove in that short term
where a really short work period exists,
to make it
more equitable
in a way that keeps the clarity that the bill aims for.
That's all I've got for you.
Great. Thank you so much. I just want to say I really appreciate the constructive

detail and
live examples are really helpful for us to work through.
So thank you for that. And we'll

Williams with us online.
Okay.
Welcome. We have 10 minutes with you.
That includes time for question, and we will leave it to

All right. Kia ora koutou. Ko Tali Williams toku ingoa.
I'm the Assistant National Secretary of Unite Union, based in Tāmaki
Makaurau.
Unite Union represents 5,500 members in fast food,

Under this bill, the work completed outside of contracted hours would

These workers are our members in low-paid industries.
And further, these workers are already in precarious employment
situations. These changes will only make life worse for
them. We don't accept that the bill is about simplifying
complex law. The findings and recommendations of the Holidays
Act Taskforce, which was put in place

The exploring of the hours-based accrual system was

they realized it financially disadvantaged a substantial number
of workers compared to the current act.
That seems to be no concern to the current government.
As an example of affected industries, a substantive number of
workers in fast food have a set amount of guaranteed
hours, and then hours beyond that are considered
additional hours, as defined in the proposed bill.
So over a sample of a 13-week period in
2025, to give an example, a third
of hours worked over that period were not guaranteed under one
main fast food brand that we cover.
A worker within this fast food brand worked an average
of 36 hours a week. However, only five of
those hours were guaranteed, with the others being classed as
additional hours.
That means under the new bill, this worker would only accrue leave
based on her five guaranteed hours,
which would be 0.38 of an hour per week
accrued. This is unfair and untenable.
To get one week's leave, this worker
would have to work for two years.
This for a worker who averages 36 hours and contributes to our
economy, pays her taxes, and so on.
The proposed 12% in lieu of leave only confirms the lack of
understanding of the hand-to-mouth nature of those working in
precarious and low-paid employment.
One in such a position cannot afford to put money aside to
allow for leave to be taken at a later date.
People will invariably need and use the money now, particularly
in a cost of living crisis.
If you don't have the savings because you're trying to cover weekly
costs, you are punished again with not being able to
afford to take the leave.
Then, of course, there's the reduction to sick leave.
The same group of fast food workers that I referred to earlier, under
this bill, would accrue sick leave for only two-thirds of the work that they
do.
Of course, the major issue with less sick leave for these workers
is that they will come to work sick because they can't afford to
take unpaid leave.
This means passing on illness, resulting in more sickness, not
only on site with their fellow workers,
but the broader community, because these people work with food
and prepare and serve food.
As we all know and have experienced, people are getting sick longer
and more seriously these days with viruses such as COVID, RSV,
and so on.
We know that under this bill, employers, seeing what seems on the face of
it to be a financial advantage, will seek to offer the least amount of
guaranteed hours and then simply top up hours as they
wish.
This means the less they will have to afford for your annual and sick leave.
This risk of increased casualization will have a compounding
negative effect on the community.
Of course, the more precarious the work, the worse off the workers'
lives, but also the less resources people have to put back
into the economy.
Precarious work also results in more exploitation and risk
of exploitation as people clamber for the dregs of hours their
employer makes available, and those workers feel compromised
to raise various workplace concerns,
and even
a fear of raising serious health and safety risks.
These are people already struggling on low wages and low hours during
a cost of living crisis.
It is shameful that the government is not only refusing to help
those most in need, but is actively seeking to legislate to make life
tougher for them.
Working people deserve holidays and time with their families, and they
deserve to take the time off they need when they're sick and when their children
are sick.
Unite Union strongly opposes this attack on leave entitlements
of the most vulnerable workers. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Tali. We have our first question from Jan Tinetti.
Kia ora, Tali, and thank you very much for your submission this morning,
or this afternoon. And can I also say thank you for
your, uh
Example that you gave, your case study that you titled there
SS, I felt that was really, really helpful.
When I was going through and we were determining Labor's position on this

rationale was that nobody would be worse off at the end than at the

And when I first started, I was told it's only a few workers,

5,500 workers. How many of them would be in this

on their roster?
I would say about half, conservatively.
Yeah. So it's quite a big amount that we're talking about.
And the question that I asked the Law Society before, is there a
fear that the way that this is written at the moment
incentivizes that to happen more?
Yes, absolutely. That is our fear.
Yeah. Okay, thank you. And question now from Teanau Tuiono.
Kia ora, Tally. Thank you very much for your very clear
submission, and with a lot of really useful detail
in there.
I wanted to come back to your point around the hours accrual system, and
you were referencing, I think, the task force in 2018.
I wasn't anywhere near Parliament then.
I wonder if you could
maybe tell us a bit more about the problems with that approach,
why it was abandoned, and what that impact of that will be on
your members.
Can I just, before you do that, Tally, just let you know you've got one minute left
in your submission. So if we could make that brief.
Okay. Well, in summary, I don't have a
whole lot to say on that other than my understanding is
that the findings of the task force were that the hours-based accrual system
wouldn't work, that it wasn't appropriate for all forms of work,
particularly those part-timers and casuals,
and therefore, that was abandoned.
I don't have any more to say on that.
Thank you. I really appreciate your participation in this process, and you making
the time to come and join us for our hearings today, Tally.
Thank you again.
And we'll now move into private session with our
officials. Thank you.
